articles.title: If Claude Fable stops helping you, you'll never know
articles.summary: If Claude Fable stops helping you, you'll never know how your models are performing or if there are any issues with their training data or configuration. The Anthropic safeguards that limit Claude's effectiveness for requests targeting frontier LLM development will be enforced through methods such as prompt modification and steering vectors, which can silently nerf the model's performance without warning to users. This creates a supply chain risk for businesses, where companies may train embedding models, rerankers, and small llms without being transparent about their use of Anthropic's safeguards, potentially leading to poor or incorrect advice given by development tools like Claude.

articles.title: Exif Smuggling
articles.summary: Exif Smuggling is a proof-of-concept evolution of cache smuggling, where an executable payload is concealed within a JPG's Exif data to passively download the payload using image caching. This allows for passive download of payloads without making any internet requests, and can be used as a second stage payload in phishing attacks.

articles.title: Upcoming breaking changes for NPM v12
articles.summary: The upcoming NPM v12 version introduces security-related changes to the `npm install` command, allowing users to opt-in instead of opting-out for certain scripts. This change affects how npm resolves dependencies from Git, remote URLs, and files, and requires users to manually approve or deny scripts using tools like `npm approve-scripts`. These changes are available as warnings in npm 11.16.0 or later, providing a transition period before the full upgrade is implemented.

articles.title: Grit: Rewriting Git in Rust with Agents
articles.summary: Grit: rewriting Git in Rust with agents has successfully ported all of Git to library-first, memory-safe Rust and passed the entire C Git test suite. A few months ago, researchers used agents to rewrite Git from scratch to be library-based, and it was able to accomplish something that GitHub had been dreaming about for 15 years. The new Grit project is a pure-Rust core library that can faithfully interact with Git repositories, canonically, and has an independent CLI surface implementing the same library in order to pass as much of the test suite as possible.

articles.title: Ultrafast machine learning on FPGAs via Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks
articles.summary: Ultrafast machine learning on FPGAs via Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks is a technique that enables ultrafast inference and online learning using the Kolmogorov-Arnold Network architecture. This approach involves designing hardware architectures for ultrafast inference and online learning using FPGAs, which are reconfigurable digital logic devices ideal for custom hardware acceleration. By leveraging the KAN architecture, developers can create highly parallel execution models that support extremely high throughput, making them well-suited for applications requiring sub-microsecond latency and high hardware efficiency.

articles.title: The LD_DEBUG environment variable (2012)
articles.summary: The LD_DEBUG environment variable allows developers to dump debug information when loading shared libraries, making it easier to resolve issues with dynamic linker problems. By setting the variable and specifying a filename, developers can direct the debugging output into a file instead of standard output, providing valuable insights into library loading processes. This feature is particularly useful for large systems with many dynamically loaded libraries, where identifying the source of errors can be challenging.

articles.title: Property Graphs
articles.summary: Property Graphs are a way to represent database contents using an alternative approach to relational structures such as tables, allowing for graph querying operations instead of join queries. PostgreSQL implements SQL/PGQ which is part of the SQL standard, defining property graphs as read-only views over relational tables. This model extends the well-known mathematical structure with user data labels and properties, enabling complex definitions and more efficient querying.

articles.title: CSS: Unavoidable Bad Parts
articles.summary: CSS is a vast and complex technology that requires a significant amount of time and experience to master. Modern web development has a relatively small subset of CSS that can be learned for simple tasks, but there are many gotchas and bad practices that can lead to frustrating debugging experiences. HTML5 semantic tag names like `main`, `article`, and `nav` make it easier to structure pages, while the lack of explicit list elements like `ul` and `dl` makes it harder to implement layouts. Additionally, CSS layout is a struggle in many GUI frameworks, with different systems using varying heuristics to arrange boxes on the screen.

articles.title: Flat Datacenter Networks at Scale at Amazon
articles.summary: Researchers have been exploring "optimal routing" networks since the late 1970s, with mathematicians defining expanders that guarantee no subset of vertices can be isolated from the rest. In recent years, advances in number theory and computer simulations have led to breakthroughs in constructing optimal expanders, but these designs are complex and only work for specific network sizes and degrees. Meanwhile, the networking industry has built on a fat-tree topology with two or more layers of switches, which was first proposed by Albert Greenberg in 2009. However, this approach remains hierarchical and rigid, making it difficult to scale up to random graphs and data center networks.
